content.full_text:
Fern Polrrrcel PRACTTcEs CouurssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA g5gl4_232g
(916) 322_5660 o Fax (916) 322_0886
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our file No. 4-09-086
Dear Ms. Jackson:
Mary Jackson
Councilmember
City of Turlock
ll29 La Sambra
Turlock, CA 95380
Aprll29,2009
FACTS
This letter re.s.r.on$1to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").' rniilltt"r is based on the facts
presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (he "Commission,,) does not act as
the finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re ogiesby (rg75) I Fppc ops. 71.) Arso,
please note that our advice is based solely on the p"rovii*r or the Act. we therefore
offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as
common law conflict of interest or Govemment code section 10g0.
QUESTION
Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit you from participating in
decisions the Turlock city council will make rega.dirg ttr city,s General plan based on
your parents' ownership of property outside the bity oiturlocl (the ,.City,,;, but inside
the city's "sphere of influenie.;
CONCLUSION
No' You do not currently have an economic interest in your parents, property and
will not until both parents are deceased and therefore ,;r;; not have a conflict of
interest.
You are a member of the Turlock city Council. The city council is beginning the
process of reviewing the city's General Plan. Your parents own property in the ..sphere
' The Political Retbrm Act is contained in Government code Sections g I 000 through g I 0 14. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
PoliticalPractices Commission are contained in sections IsI r0 ilr;;;; Ig997 of ritle 2 of the California
code of Regulations' AII regulatory ref'erences are to Titre 2. Divisioi- 6 of the Califbrnia code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
!a:=,


File No. 4-09-086
Page No. 2
of influence" of Turlock's General Plan, but not within the city limits. you and your
sister are named in living trust and when both your parents urc d."rurrd, you will become
joint owners of the property. Neither you nor you. iirte, has a right to consume or invade
the principal of the trust; the trust is not irrevoiable.
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials ,.perform
their duties in an impartial manner, frie from bias caused uy ttreir own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them," (Section gl00l(b).)
Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her oriiciat position to influenc" u!ou"-rrr'"ntut
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
The Commillion has adopted an eight-step standard analysis to decide whether an
official has a disqualifying confliit of interist. (Regulation lg70b@xl)_(8).) The
general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest.iirtr whenever a pufri" orncial makes
a govemmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one
or more of his or her financial interests. (section g7103.)
Step One: Are you a public Officiat?
As a member of the Turlock City Council, you are a public official under the Act.
(Section 82048.)
step Two: wilt you-be making, participating in making, or using or attempting to
use your official position to influence a goyernmental decision?
The city council will be reviewing and making decisions about the city,s General
Plan. consequently, you may not make, participate ii making, or otherwis" u'r" you,
official position to influence any decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on any of your economic interests. (Regulations lg702.l-
t8702.4.)
step Three: Do you have a financial interest in the decisions at issue?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
Section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or helimmediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests. (section 87103; Regurationr tszo:-tsiol.s.1 The appricable economic
interests include:
1. An interest in a business entity in which a public official has a
direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (section g7103(a),
Regulation lg703.l(a).) An interest in any business entity in
which a public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section
87 103(d), Regulation 1 8703. l(b).)


File No. 4-09-086
Page No. 3
2. An interest in real property in which a public official has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 o, *ori. (Section gZl03(b),
Regulation 18703.2.)
3. Any source of income, including promised income, to the
public official that aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months
prior ro the decision. (section g7103(c), Regulation 1g703.3.)
O. 1r, source of gifts to the public official if the gifts aggregate to
$420 or more within 12 months prior to the decisi6n. tsi-ctiJn
87 103(e), Regulation tg7 03.4.)
5. A public official also has an economic interest in his or her
personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of
his or her immediate family. This is also known as the ,.personal
financial effects" rule. (section g7103, Regulation 1g7d3.5.)
As a named beneficiary to your parents' trust ("living will"), you do not currently
have an "economic interest" in the property listed in tire trust. Regulation lgz34explains
that an official has an interest in real property held by a trust if the official is a beneficiary
and presently receives income or has an irrevocabte iigtrt to receive income on principal.
(See Regulation 18234, copy enclosed.) Your situatioi does not apply to this provision.
- 
Because you do not have an economic interest in the real property that is within
the "sphere of influence" of Turlock's general plan, we do not upply *d analyze the
remaining steps in the analysis.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
,drfr4^/,t(lN|r\
By: Heather M. Rowan
Counsel, Legal Division
HMRjgl
Enclosure

joint owners of the property. Neither you nor you. iirte, has a right to consume or invade
the principal of the trust; the trust is not irrevoiable.
